nativeRange.habitat: Found on shallow coastal reefs and outer-reef drop-offs, typically at depths of 0–15 m, though the species has been recorded to 50 m. In the wild it tends to hover close to coral or rocky rubble and is usually seen alone or in small, loosely scattered groups.

diet.notes: Generally considered an unfussy micropredator that has been reported to readily accept most prepared aquarium foods, including mysis shrimp, brine shrimp, finely chopped meaty foods (krill, shrimp, squid, clam, mussel), and quality marine flake or pellet preparations. Has been documented preying on small crustaceans and nuisance bristleworms in the display. Generally advised to feed 2–3 small meals per day with varied, pigment-rich foods to help maintain its purple-and-yellow coloration.

summary: The Bicolor Dottyback is a small, vivid Western Pacific reef fish with a striking purple-and-yellow pattern. Despite its beginner-friendly hardiness, it is widely considered aggressive and territorial, and is best kept one per tank with plenty of rockwork for cover.
careNotes:
The Bicolor Dottyback is generally considered one of the hardiest beginner-level saltwater fish — it adapts readily to captive foods, tolerates a broad parameter window, and is commonly available from both wild-caught and aquacultured sources. Its physical hardiness makes it an accessible first marine fish, but the aggressive temperament is a significant separate consideration that requires careful planning when selecting tankmates.

Conspecific aggression is reported as severe — keep one per tank, and avoid housing it with the similarly shaped and colored Royal Gramma (Gramma loreto), which it may attack relentlessly. It should not be combined with other dottybacks either. Reef compatibility is generally considered good with corals and most sessile invertebrates, but the species has been reliably reported to prey on small ornamental shrimp and other mobile micro-invertebrates, so it is best regarded as reef-safe with caution.

Plenty of live rock with numerous caves, crevices, and overhangs is required to give it a defendable home territory. A tank of at least 150 L (about 40 US gallons) is recommended to allow the Bicolor Dottyback to establish its territory while still giving more timid tankmates enough space to stay out of its claimed zone.

The older scientific name Pictichromis paccagnellae is still the most widely used in the aquarium trade and hobbyist literature; the name Pseudochromis paccagnellae appears as a synonym from an earlier classification.
breedingNotes: Has been reported to breed in captivity. As with related species, the male is generally expected to prepare a cave or crevice and court a female, who deposits an egg ball inside. Captive-bred specimens are available from some suppliers, though consistent commercial production has not been as firmly established as for certain other dottyback species. Raising fry is considered moderately challenging due to the small size of the larvae.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Used for confirmed scientific name (Pictichromis paccagnellae), care level (Easy/beginner), temperament (Semi-Aggressive — defends territory against fish 2–3x its size), reef-safe (Yes), diet (carnivore — meaty foods, frozen, bristleworm predator), max size (3 in), minimum tank (30 gal), water parameters (72–78°F, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025, dKH 8–12), family Pseudochromidae, and the bicolor (anterior purple, posterior yellow) description.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy (family Pseudochromidae, order Ovalentaria/misc), accepted scientific name Pictichromis paccagnellorum (Axelrod, 1973) — paccagnellae retained as primary name to match trade usage and original description, max length 7.0 cm TL, depth 0–50 m (typically 0–15 m), wild temperature 25.8–29.3°C, native distribution (Indonesia: Java and Sulawesi; Vanuatu; Palau), IUCN Least Concern.

additional.name: TFH Magazine
additional.notes: Third source for temperament (extremely aggressive/territorial, 'grouper-size attitude', will fight conspecifics to death and may nip fins of larger tankmates), confirmed beginner-hardiness despite aggression, max size 7 cm (2¾ in), reef-safe with caution (hunts small crustaceans), readily accepts captive foods, has been bred in captivity. Source for the alsoKnownAs list (royal dottyback, royal pseudochromis, bicolor cichlops, false gramma).

additional.name: reefapp.net
additional.notes: Fourth source for max size (7 cm), temperature (~26°C), salinity (~1.026 SG), threat-to-shrimp/crustacean rating (reef safe with caution), and the jumping behavior that justifies the escapeRisk: high rating and recommendation for a tightly-sealed lid.

fish.breedingNotes: The Bicolor Dottyback is a cave-spawning species. The male prepares a hiding site and courts the female, who deposits an egg ball inside the cave. The species has been bred in captivity, though captive-bred specimens are not consistently available commercially. Raising fry is considered moderately challenging due to the small larval size.
